title: Vinegret
subtitle: Russian beetroot and vegetable salad
excerpt: Diced cooked beetroot, potato, carrot, and gherkin with sauerkraut and red onion, dressed in sunflower oil and vinegar. Russia's oldest and most elemental salad, the colour of a bruise, the taste of a root cellar.
sourceNotes: Vinegret (from the French vinaigrette) is one of the oldest Russian salads, predating Olivier by decades. It is a Lenten staple, entirely vegan as traditionally made, and was eaten across the Russian Empire as a cold side dish or first course during periods of religious fasting. The combination of cooked root vegetables, pickled cucumbers, and sauerkraut dressed in oil and vinegar reflects the larder of nineteenth-century Russia: preserved and root vegetables that lasted through winter.

content.text: Vinegret stains everything it touches a deep purple-red, and this is the point: the cooked beetroot bleeds into the other vegetables, dyeing the potatoes pink and the gherkins magenta, until the whole salad is unified by colour. This does not happen immediately. It takes an hour in the fridge. Serve it the next day and the colour transformation is complete.

If you want to delay the bleeding and keep the potato white against the red, dress the beetroot separately with a little oil before combining. This is the neater option for a dinner table; the all-purple version is the more traditional one.

items.name: Beetroot
items.prepNote: cooked, peeled, and diced into 1 cm cubes

items.name: Potato
items.prepNote: boiled whole, cooled, peeled, and diced

items.name: Carrot
items.prepNote: boiled whole, cooled, peeled, and diced

content.text: Ensure all cooked vegetables are completely cold. Combine the {{beetroot}}, {{potato}}, {{carrot}}, {{gherkins}}, {{sauerkraut}}, and {{red-onion}} in a large bowl and mix gently. Whisk together the {{sunflower-oil}}, {{cider-vinegar}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{black-pepper-ground}}. Pour the dressing over the salad and turn gently to coat. Taste and adjust salt and vinegar. Refrigerate for at least 30 minutes before serving.

items.fix: Pat all vegetables dry before combining. The gherkins and sauerkraut in particular should be squeezed of excess liquid
items.cause: Vegetables not properly drained or dried after cooking

items.fix: Add another teaspoon of vinegar or a little gherkin brine from the jar. Taste after every addition. The salad should taste clean and slightly sharp
Cook the vegetables until just tender, not falling apart. Cool completely before dicing, hot vegetables crumble when cut

content.text: Vinegret is the Lenten salad and the winter salad of Russian cooking, made from preserved and stored vegetables at a time of year when fresh produce was unavailable. It appears alongside Olivier at festive tables, at weekday meals as a cold side dish, and at any gathering where a vegetable course is needed. Unlike Olivier, which is universally beloved, vinegret divides people: the earthiness of the beetroot and sauerkraut together is either deeply satisfying or too much. Those who love it eat large quantities.
